title: What the Network Actually Predicts
concept: Rather than predicting the clean image, the diffusion network is trained to predict the specific noise that was added at a given timestep.

exercises.prompt: When a photo gets 'noisy' or grainy, what does that mean?

options.text: Random speckly static is mixed into the picture

exercises.explain: Noise is random speckly static mixed into an image, like TV static covering a picture.

cards.title: Guess the Static, Not the Picture!
cards.body: You might think an AI image generator paints the picture directly. Surprise: it actually spends most of its time playing detective, trying to figure out exactly what static was sprinkled onto an image!

cards.title: What Gets Predicted?
cards.body: A diffusion network isn't trained to output the clean image. Instead, at each noisy step, it's trained to predict the exact noise pattern that was added to create that noisy version. Once it knows the noise, it can subtract it to reveal a cleaner image.

cards.title: The Crumb Detective
cards.body: Imagine someone sprinkled crumbs on a cookie photo. Instead of trying to redraw the whole cookie from scratch, a smart detective just figures out exactly which specks are crumbs. Remove the crumbs, and the cookie appears! The network is that crumb detective, spotting the 'noise crumbs', not repainting the cookie.

cards.title: One Step in Action
cards.body: A picture of a cat gets a splash of random noise added, becoming blurry and grainy. The network looks at this noisy cat and outputs a map of the exact noise splash that was added — not a new cat picture. Subtracting that noise map from the grainy image nudges it back toward the clean cat photo.

cards.title: Myth: The AI Paints Directly
cards.body: Myth: 'The network looks at noise and draws the final picture in one shot.' Truth: It predicts only the noise that was added at that step. The clean image is revealed gradually by removing predicted noise over many small steps, not by painting the whole scene at once.

cards.title: Predict Image vs. Predict Noise
cards.body: These are two different training goals — diffusion models choose the noise-prediction path because it's simpler and more stable to learn.

left.text: Network tries to guess the whole clean picture directly from noisy input — a much harder, blurrier target.

right.text: Network only guesses the noise pattern added at that step — a simpler, more precise target that's easier to learn.

exercises.prompt: What does a diffusion network actually learn to predict during training?

options.text: The exact noise that was added to the image
The name of the object in the picture
The size of the original image file

exercises.explain: The network's job is to predict the specific noise pattern added at that timestep, so it can be subtracted to clean up the image.

exercises.prompt: The diffusion network is trained to directly output the final clean image in one step.
exercises.explain: False! It predicts the noise added at each step, and the clean image emerges gradually as that predicted noise is removed over many steps.

exercises.sentence: The diffusion network is trained to predict the specific ___ that was added at a given timestep.
exercises.explain: The network predicts the noise added at that timestep, not the image itself, its color, or its size.

exercises.prompt: Once the network predicts the noise in a noisy image, what happens next to move toward a clean picture?

options.text: The predicted noise is subtracted from the noisy image
The predicted noise is added again to make it noisier
The image is deleted and a new random one is made

exercises.explain: Subtracting the predicted noise from the noisy image nudges it closer to the clean, original-looking image.

exercises.prompt: Boss Challenge: Why do diffusion models train the network to predict the added noise instead of predicting the clean image directly?

options.text: Because predicting noise is a simpler, more precise target that's easier and more stable to learn
Because noise prediction uses less computer memory than any other method
Because clean images are illegal to use in training
Because the noise is always the same for every image, so it's easy to memorize

exercises.explain: Predicting the added noise gives the network a simpler, more consistent target than trying to guess an entire complex clean image at once, making training more stable and effective.
